channel.title: Nature Communications
channel.description:
Nature Communications is an open access, multidisciplinary journal dedicated to publishing high-quality research in all areas of the biological, health, physical, chemical and Earth sciences. Papers published by the journal aim to represent important advances of significance to specialists within each field.

We are committed to providing an efficient service for both authors and readers. Our team of independent editors make rapid and fair publication decisions. Prompt dissemination of accepted papers to a wide readership and beyond is achieved through a programme of continuous online publication.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Thin film lithium niobate on sapphire for integrated mid-infrared modulator
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 21 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-69880-5

We demonstrate a mid-infrared Mach-Zehnder modulator operating from 3.95 to 4.5 μ m, achieving >20 GHz bandwidth, 17 dB extinction ratio, VπL = 22 V ⋅ cm implemented on lithium niobate on sapphire platform. The device is used to demonstrate 10 Gbit s−1 data transmission, and a 70 GHz broad frequency comb.
item.dc:title: Thin film lithium niobate on sapphire for integrated mid-infrared modulator
item.dc:creator: Pierre Didier
Prakhar Jain
Mathieu Bertrand
Jost Kellner
Oliver Pitz
Zhecheng Dai
Tristan Kuttner
Mattias Beck
Baile Chen
Jérôme Faist
Rachel Grange

item.title: Liquid photonic-molecule microlasers for ultrasensitive biosensing
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 21 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-69840-z

Droplet microlasers are emerging as powerful tools for label-free biosensing. Here, the authors demonstrate a new method for creating liquid photonic-molecule microlasers that combine low lasing threshold and enhanced sensitivity, achieving biomolecular detection limit down to attoMolar level.
item.dc:title: Liquid photonic-molecule microlasers for ultrasensitive biosensing
item.dc:creator: Yan Wang
Yu-Hao Hu
Jin-Lei Wu
Rui Duan
Ya-Feng Jiao
Hai-Yan Wang
Li-Ying Jiang
Le-Man Kuang
Han-Dong Sun
Hui Jing

item.title: Localized quasiparticles in a fluxonium with quasi-two-dimensional amorphous kinetic inductors
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 21 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-69709-1

High kinetic inductance materials are widely used in superconducting circuits, yet their loss mechanisms are not fully understood. Here the authors study quantum circuits incorporating nanowires made of quasi-2D disordered superconductor WSi and identify localized quasiparticles as the dominant source of loss.
item.dc:title: Localized quasiparticles in a fluxonium with quasi-two-dimensional amorphous kinetic inductors
item.dc:creator: Trevyn F. Q. Larson
Sarah Garcia Jones
Tamás Kalmár
Pablo Aramburu Sanchez
Sai Pavan Chitta
Varun Verma
Kristen L. Genter
Stephen T. Gill
Katarina Cicak
Sae Woo Nam
Gergö Fülöp
Jens Koch
Raymond W. Simmonds
András Gyenis
